reusable.what: Self-contained LLM social-metadata generator. Takes (video_subject, video_script, language, platform) and returns a fixed dict {title, caption, hashtags:[...]} with per-platform length/hashtag-count caps, JSON-with-code-fence-recovery parsing, and a heuristic fallback when the LLM is down. This is exactly ClipPilot's missing 'metadata' stage and it already drives the YouTube upload path.
reusable.notes: MIT so import is legal, BUT _generate_response is bound to MoneyPrinter's config.toml provider zoo (40+ branches). For ClipPilot, PORT the pure pieces and swap the model call for your existing Claude vision brain. Reusable as-is: the per-platform spec table SOCIAL_PLATFORMS = {'tiktok':{'title_max':100,'caption_max':2200,'hashtag_count':5},'youtube_shorts':{'title_max':100,'caption_max':5000,'hashtag_count':3},'instagram_reels':{'title_max':125,'caption_max':2200,'hashtag_count':8},'facebook_reels':{'title_max':125,'caption_max':2200,'hashtag_count':5}}. The prompt contract: 'Respond ONLY with a single valid minified JSON object ... keys: title, caption, hashtags ... hashtags: a JSON array of exactly N strings each starting with # no spaces'. Reuse verbatim: _normalize_hashtags() (regex re.sub(r'[^\w]','',item,flags=re.UNICODE), dedupe by .lower(), prefix '#', cap to count) and _parse_social_metadata()'s recovery: try json.loads(_strip_code_fence(r)); except, re.search(r'\{.*\}', r, re.DOTALL). Feed it the existing understand_video output as video_script.

reusable.what: Complete multi-platform publisher: one multipart POST cross-posts a single mp4 to TikTok + Instagram + YouTube Shorts + Facebook via the Upload-Post.com SaaS gateway. Solves ClipPilot's hardest missing stage (OAuth to 3 platforms) by delegating to one API key — no per-platform OAuth, no google-api-python-client. Pure requests + stdlib, Windows/CPU-clean.
reusable.notes: MIT, zero heavy deps (requests only) — drop the file in directly and replace config.app.get() with your settings. Exact API call shape: POST https://api.upload-post.com/api/upload, headers={'Authorization':'Apikey <KEY>'}, multipart files={'video':<fh>}, data as repeated tuples: ('user',username),('title',title[:2200]),('privacy_level','PUBLIC_TO_EVERYONE'), then one ('platform[]', p) per platform. YouTube extras only when 'youtube' in platforms: ('youtube_title', t[:100]),('youtube_description',d),('tags[]',tag) per tag,('privacyStatus','public'|'unlisted'),('containsSyntheticMedia','true'). timeout=300. Response: {'success':bool,'request_id':...}. Status poll: GET /api/uploadposts/status?request_id=. Caveat: Upload-Post is a PAID 3rd-party SaaS (needs api_key+username) — it is NOT a free direct uploader; if you want first-party uploads you'd still write the YouTube Data API path yourself. containsSyntheticMedia is hardcoded 'true' for AI-content disclosure.

reusable.what: The end-to-end wiring recipe: after final render, if upload is configured+auto, generate youtube_shorts metadata via llm.generate_social_metadata, build youtube_extra={youtube_title,youtube_description,tags,privacyStatus,containsSyntheticMedia}, then loop final_video_paths calling cross_post_video. Shows exactly how metadata + publish stages connect.
reusable.notes: Design template for ClipPilot's post-render step in the SQLite job engine. Key shape: youtube_extra = {'youtube_title': metadata.get('title', subject), 'youtube_description': metadata.get('caption',''), 'tags': metadata.get('hashtags',[]), 'privacyStatus': <cfg>, 'containsSyntheticMedia': True}. Note it generates metadata with platform='youtube_shorts' specifically (for the 100-char title cap) while passing video_subject as the generic TikTok/IG title. Collect per-video results into cross_post_results for the job record.

reusable.what: Free CPU-only TTS via edge_tts (Microsoft Edge online voices) with WordBoundary events converted into SRT subtitle timing — directly usable for Section B (script->TTS) AND for word-timed captions without re-running whisper on the TTS output.
reusable.notes: edge_tts is free, no API key, no GPU, Windows-fine (it's an online MS endpoint, so needs network). create_edge_tts_communicate() probes Communicate signature and passes boundary='WordBoundary' to get per-word timing; stream_edge_tts_chunks() consumes stream_sync() with a daemon-thread timeout wrapper (good defensive pattern). The WordBoundary->SubMaker->get_srt() flow gives you script-synced caption timestamps for free in the faceless-funnel pipeline. SiliconFlow/Azure-v2 branches are paid/key-gated — ignore those, take only the edge_tts path. Python 3.14: verify edge_tts wheel availability.

best_for_clippilot: app\services\upload_post.py — the UploadPostService.upload_video cross-poster. It is a tiny (137-line), MIT-licensed, requests-only module that collapses ClipPilot's entire unbuilt PUBLISHING stage (TikTok + Instagram/Reels + YouTube Shorts + Facebook, with AI-synthetic-media disclosure and YouTube title/description/tags) into one multipart POST, sidestepping three separate OAuth integrations. Import it nearly verbatim. Full path: C:\Priyansh\Money making\MoneyPrinterTurbo-main\MoneyPrinterTurbo-main\app\services\upload_post.py
do_not_use: Do NOT lift llm.py's _generate_response() — it is a 440-line tangle of 40+ provider branches (g4f/qwen/ernie/cloudflare/pollinations etc.) bound to config.toml; route ClipPilot's metadata prompt through your existing Claude brain instead. Do NOT depend on Upload-Post as if it were free — it is a PAID third-party SaaS gateway requiring api_key+username (no key = no publish); it is not a self-hosted uploader. In voice.py, skip the siliconflow_tts and azure_tts_v2 branches (paid/key-gated) — take only the free edge_tts path. Nothing here is GPL and nothing is GPU-mandatory, but everything network-dependent (edge_tts, Upload-Post, all LLM calls) needs connectivity, so they are not offline-capable.

best_for_clippilot: C:\Priyansh\Money making\ShortGPT-stable\ShortGPT-stable\shortGPT\gpt\gpt_yt.py (generate_title_description_dict) together with its prompt at shortGPT\prompt_templates\yt_title_description.yaml — a complete, drop-in social METADATA generator (title + description + hashtags) that ClipPilot has not built yet. Port both: feed the Claude understanding/transcript as <<CONTENT>>, keep the loop-until-valid-JSON pattern, and route the LLM call through ClipPilot's existing Claude brain instead of OpenAI/Gemini.
do_not_use: Do NOT import the rendering engine: shortGPT\editing_framework\core_editing_engine.py and editing_engine.py are MoviePy-based (CompositeVideoClip/TextClip, moviepy==2.1.2 API with .with_start/.with_effects) — ClipPilot already has real ffmpeg clip/caption(libass)/compose, so this is redundant and adds a heavy MoviePy+numpy dependency. reusable.what: Generates a YouTube/Shorts title + description (with hashtags) from a transcript/understanding. Exactly ClipPilot's missing METADATA stage. Loops until the LLM returns valid JSON with title+description keys.
reusable.notes: Port the body, swap llm_completion for ClipPilot's Claude call. Core: out={'title':'','description':''}; chat,system=load_yaml('yt_title_description.yaml'); chat=chat.replace('<<CONTENT>>',content); while not out['title'] or not out['description']: r=llm(chat_prompt=chat,system=system,temp=1); resp=json.loads(r); out.update(resp). Pair with the YAML prompt (next item). Guard the while-loop with a max-retry counter — original can spin forever if the model never emits valid JSON.

reusable.what: The actual prompt that produces catchy <80-char title (max 2 emojis) + <=240-char description with hashtags as strict JSON. This is the load-bearing IP for metadata.
reusable.notes: system_prompt verbatim: 'You are a youtube shorts title and description expert writer... Use only MAXIMUM 2 emojis in the title... use hashtags in the description. The title has to be less than 80 characters... description maximum 240 characters... give the title and description in a perfect json format... key `title` and `description`. In your JSON, use the double quotes "" instead of ''.' chat_prompt is just '<<CONTENT>>'. Tighten to instruct a dedicated `hashtags` array if ClipPilot wants structured tags for cross-posting.

reusable.what: Prompt + parser that turn timed captions into 4-5s segments each with three 1-2 word concrete visual B-ROLL search queries (monetization-safe). Directly feeds a B-roll/stock-footage fetch stage.
reusable.notes: Prompt enforces: ONLY English, 1-2 words, concrete visual nouns/actions, family-friendly, output strict JSON {video_segments:[{time_range:[s,e],queries:[q1,q2,q3]}]}. Parser (gpt_editing.py L53-98) validates 0<=t0<t1<=end, pads to exactly 3 queries, retries 4x. Port the prompt + the validation loop; wire queries to a stock provider (Pexels/local library). Pairs with getImageQueryPairs (L12) for image variant.

reusable.what: Pure-python word-timing chunker: converts whisper word-level segments into on-screen caption lines split by maxCaptionSize, punctuation boundaries, and a 5-word cap. Useful for ClipPilot's richer styled-caption timing even though ClipPilot burns via libass.
reusable.notes: No heavy deps (only re). getCaptionsWithTime(whisper, maxCaptionSize=15) returns [((start,end), 'TEXT'), ...] ready to emit as ASS Dialogue lines. ClipPilot can import this to decide line breaks/timing, then format each tuple into its own ASS style instead of MoviePy. should_split logic keys on len>maxCaptionSize OR trailing punctuation OR len(line)>=5 words.

reusable.what: The concrete caption look ShortGPT ships: big bold centered word-chunk with stroke outline. Gives ClipPilot ready style values to translate into an ASS style line.
reusable.notes: Style params: font=fonts/LuckiestGuy-Regular.ttf, font_size=100, color=white, stroke_width=3, stroke_color=black, method=caption, size=[900,450], position=center, text uppercased by caller (text.upper()). Translate to libass: Fontname=Luckiest Guy,Fontsize=100,PrimaryColour=&H00FFFFFF,OutlineColour=&H00000000,Outline=3,Alignment=5,Bold=1. LuckiestGuy font ships in repo fonts/ (check its OFL license before bundling).

reusable.what: Theme->script generator for faceless/ad-share shorts (Section B/C): generateFactSubjects(n) invents original 'X facts' themes, generateFacts(type) writes a <140-word hook+facts script. The script->TTS->captions->compose ordering (content_short_engine.stepDict 1-12) is the exact funnel ClipPilot needs.
reusable.notes: Use the step ORDER from content_short_engine.py (generateScript->TTS->speedUp->timeCaptions->imageSearchTerms->imageUrls->bgMusic->bgVideo->render->metadata) as ClipPilot's Section B/C state machine. facts_generator.yaml system_prompt gives a strong few-shot 'hook then facts, <50s/~140 words, captivating+original' template. facts_subjects_generation.yaml: 'pick a random subject, be original, `Subject` facts format, json-parseable array' for Section C theme seeding. Swap llm_completion for Claude; temp 1.3-1.69 in originals for variety.

reusable.what: Free, CPU-only, offline-capable TTS via Microsoft edge-tts (no API key, unlimited chars). Fills the TTS step of Section B faceless-funnel generation without a paid voice API.
reusable.notes: edge-tts is permissive and pip-installable on Windows/py3.14. Core async: communicate=edge_tts.Communicate(text, voiceName); async for chunk in communicate.stream(): if chunk['type']=='audio': file.write(chunk['data']). Note the repo's event-loop wrapper (run_in_executor over run_until_complete) is awkward and can race; for ClipPilot just call asyncio.run(communicate.save(outputfile)) directly. Voice name e.g. 'en-US-AriaNeural'. get_remaining_characters returns infinity (unmetered).

reusable.symbol: StandardPipeline (setup_environment / generate_content / determine_title / plan_visuals / initialize_storyboard / produce_assets / post_production / finalize)
reusable.what: End-to-end theme->video orchestration: topic -> LLM narrations -> (optional) image/video prompts -> per-frame TTS+render -> concat+BGM. This IS the Section B faceless-funnel and Section C ad-share generation skeleton (script->TTS->visuals->compose), already wired as an async template-method lifecycle. mode='generate' (LLM writes the script from a theme) and mode='fixed' (use a supplied script verbatim) cover both B and C.
reusable.notes: Port the lifecycle, not the class verbatim (it depends on ComfyUI/RunningHub media services ClipPilot won't use). Key reusable shape: generate_content() calls generate_narrations_from_topic(llm, topic, n_scenes, min_words, max_words); plan_visuals() detects template_type and SKIPS media gen for static templates ('Static template - skip media generation pipeline' => faster/cheaper, no GPU) — exactly ClipPilot's CPU path; post_production() = video_service.concat_videos(segment_paths, out, bgm_path, bgm_volume, bgm_mode). Reuse the static-template branch to make a faceless funnel with zero image generation.

reusable.what: Battle-tested LLM prompt that turns a theme into N short-video storyboard narrations as strict JSON {"narrations":[...]}. Heavy anti-repetition / opening-diversity / language-consistency rules and a self-check pass. Directly drives Section B/C script generation.
reusable.notes: Single self-contained .format() template, no deps. Output contract: ```json {"narrations": ["...","..."]}``` with word count clamped to {min_words}~{max_words}. Pair with the JSON extractor _parse_json() from content_generators.py. content_narration.py is the same shape for 'expand existing content' (build_content_narration_prompt). Copy the file, keep the Apache header.

reusable.what: Stateless async helpers around the prompts: theme->narrations, content->narrations, fixed-script splitter (paragraph/line/sentence), and a robust LLM-JSON extractor with markdown-fence + brace fallback. generate_title is a ready-made METADATA generator (short attractive titles with strict char limit and trailing-punctuation cleanup).
reusable.notes: Only dep is an llm_service with signature `await llm(prompt, temperature, max_tokens) -> str` — trivially satisfied by ClipPilot's existing Claude brain. generate_title(llm, content, strategy='auto'|'direct'|'llm', max_length=15) gives titles for the metadata stage today; extend the same pattern (new prompt returning {"title":..,"description":..,"hashtags":[..]}) to cover full social metadata. _parse_json handles ```json fences, raw object, and a narrations/image_prompts-keyed regex fallback.

reusable.symbol: edge_tts(text, voice, rate, volume, pitch, output_path, retry_count, retry_base_delay) -> bytes ; speed_to_rate (in tts_voices.py) ; list_voices()
reusable.what: Production CPU-only, free, no-API-key TTS via Microsoft Edge (edge-tts SDK): 400+ voices/100+ languages, returns MP3 bytes and writes file. Includes exponential-backoff retry for 401/NoAudioReceived, certifi SSL, a per-event-loop concurrency semaphore (max 3) and pre-request jitter to dodge rate limits. This is the TTS engine for Sections B and C.
reusable.notes: pip install edge-tts certifi aiohttp. Core call: communicate = edge_tts_sdk.Communicate(text, voice, rate, volume, pitch); async for chunk in communicate.stream(): if chunk['type']=='audio': audio_chunks.append(chunk['data']). voice format used elsewhere is the raw ShortName e.g. 'zh-CN-YunjianNeural' / 'en-US-JennyNeural'; rate is a '+20%' string from speed_to_rate(speed). The 401-retry + semaphore logic is the load-bearing part to copy — Edge TTS rate-limits hard without it. (Note: edge-tts itself is GPL-3.0 as a dependency — SUBPROCESS/pip-call it, do NOT vendor its source; this wrapper file is Apache and safe to copy.)

reusable.what: Complete ffmpeg-python compose toolkit for assembling Section B/C shorts: concat (stream-copy demuxer OR re-encoding filter), looped/faded BGM mix, static-image+audio -> mp4 segment, transparent-PNG overlay onto scaled 9:16 video, and intelligent audio/video duration reconciliation (freeze last frame vs trim vs silence-pad).
reusable.notes: pip install ffmpeg-python (needs ffmpeg on PATH; check_ffmpeg() already validates). Reusable recipes: demuxer concat => ffmpeg -f concat -safe 0 -i filelist.txt -c copy out.mp4 ; image->video => ffmpeg.input(image, loop=1, framerate=fps)+input(audio) .output(t=audio_duration, vcodec='libx264', acodec='aac', pix_fmt='yuv420p', crf=23) ; BGM loop+mix => bgm_input=ffmpeg.input(bgm, stream_loop=-1); bgm.audio.filter('volume', vol).filter('afade',type='in',duration=fi); ffmpeg.filter([video.audio,bgm],'amix',inputs=2,duration='first') ; freeze-pad => video_stream.filter('tpad', stop_mode='clone', stop_duration=pad). overlay_image_on_video gives the styled-caption compositing path (see frame_html). Pure-stdlib/ffmpeg, no GPU.

reusable.symbol: HTMLFrameGenerator.generate_frame(title, text, image, ext, output_path) / _replace_parameters / parse_template_parameters
reusable.what: The repo's 'styled captions' mechanism: render an HTML/CSS template (with {{param:type=default}} DSL and meta-tag-driven media size) to a transparent PNG via headless Playwright Chromium, then overlay it on the video. This is how Pixelle does rounded-box/kinetic-style caption+title cards WITHOUT libass/ASS — full CSS styling instead.
reusable.notes: Reference/port, not a drop-in: heavyweight (pip install playwright + `playwright install chromium`, ~spins a browser). ClipPilot already burns captions via libass which is lighter for kinetic word-timed subs. Take the IDEA for richer styled cards (per-frame title/CTA overlays for Section C ads): author a 1080x1920 HTML template with CSS, render to transparent PNG (page.screenshot(omit_background=True)), then VideoService.overlay_image_on_video(video, png, out, scale_mode='cover'). DSL: {{text}}, {{title}}, {{image}} are preset; custom {{cta:text=Shop Now}}, {{accent:color=#FF0000}} parsed by parse_template_parameters.

reusable.what: Provider-agnostic LLM wrapper over the OpenAI SDK that explicitly supports Anthropic Claude AND local Ollama (free) via base_url override, plus structured output: it injects the Pydantic model_json_schema() into the prompt and parses the reply back into the model (works on providers lacking native json_schema, e.g. Ollama/Qwen). Useful as the metadata/script generator's structured-output layer.
reusable.notes: ClipPilot already has a Claude brain, so this is mostly a reference for the schema-injection trick: enhanced_prompt = prompt + _get_json_schema_instruction(Model) where the instruction embeds json.dumps(Model.model_json_schema()) and demands 'ONLY a valid JSON object'; then _parse_response_as_model tries json.loads, then ```json fences, then first{..last}. This is the robust cross-provider path to get {title,description,hashtags} as a Pydantic object from Claude OR a local Ollama model. base_url switches Claude<->Ollama<->OpenAI. Note the docstring lists claude-sonnet-4-5/opus/haiku as supported model ids via OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

best_for_clippilot: pixelle_video\\pipelines\\standard.py (StandardPipeline) — it is the ready-made Section B/C generation orchestration (theme -> LLM narrations -> per-frame TTS -> compose+BGM) with a CPU-only static-template branch that skips all image/video generation. Port its lifecycle and wire in ClipPilot's Claude brain + the repo's edge_tts wrapper (tts_util.py) and VideoService (video.py), and you have the faceless-funnel + ad-share pipelines with no GPU and no paid APIs. The single highest-leverage supporting asset is pixelle_video\\prompts\\topic_narration.py for the actual script generation.
do_not_use: 1) Do NOT vendor the edge-tts package source — edge-tts itself is GPL-3.0; pip-install and call it (the Apache wrapper tts_util.py is fine to copy). 2) Do NOT pull in the ComfyUI/RunningHub media generation stack (services\\comfy_base_service.py, services\\api_services\\*, services\\media.py, image/video_*.py) — those are GPU/paid-cloud (DashScope, Kling, Seedance, SeeDream, GPT-image) media generators, the exact opposite of CPU-only; the asset_based/standard pipelines reference them but the static-template path avoids them. 3) frame_html.py is a Playwright/Chromium dependency (heavy, launches a browser) — reference the styling idea only; ClipPilot's existing libass caption burn is lighter. 4) There is NO publishing/uploader code and NO YouTube/Instagram/Facebook API code anywhere in this repo, and NO ASS/libass styled-caption code — do not expect to harvest the 'publish' or native-ASS 'captions' stages here; captions are done via HTML->PNG overlay, not .ass.

reusable.path: packages\cli\src\templates (whole repo is Node/TypeScript, not Python)
reusable.what: HyperFrames itself is a Node.js 22+ / TypeScript CLI (`npx hyperframes`), NOT a Python package. ClipPilot is Python 3.14. So nothing here can be pip-imported. The reusable value is (a) PORTING the deterministic data recipes to Python and (b) optionally SUBPROCESSING the `hyperframes` CLI if Node is acceptable on the box.
reusable.notes: Establishes the integration constraint: this is a JS render engine (headless Chrome + ffmpeg). For a pure-Python CPU app, prefer PORT of the algorithms below over importing. CLI requires Node 22 + FFmpeg installed.

reusable.what: The single best harvest. Pure-data algorithm that turns whisper word lists ([{text,start,end}]) into styled, render-ready, non-overlapping caption GROUPS. This is exactly ClipPilot's missing styled-caption grouping brain and is trivially portable to Python (no GPL, no GPU, no Chrome — just list math).
reusable.notes: PORT this to Python verbatim. Core recipe: (1) CLEAN: drop music ♪/♯, drop tokens with no [A-Za-z0-9], drop dur<0.05s, drop fillers {uh,um,ah,oh,huh} when dur<0.1s. (2) GROUP with hard splits: scene boundary, silence gap (word.start - prev.end > 0.18s), sentence-end punctuation /[.?!,;:—]$/ closes AFTER the word, and a DENSITY-MODULATED word cap: local words within [t,t+1.0s) >3.5→cap2, >2.5→cap3, else 4. (3) TAIL_PAD=0.12s hold on last word but clamp to next group's start (`end = Math.min(lastWord.end+0.12, nextStart)`) — prevents two groups on screen at once. (4) free emphasis tagging: is-allcaps (>=2 letters, no lowercase), is-numeric (^[0-9]). (5) overlap invariant check. Each group → {id,start,end,text,words:[{text,start,end,classes}]}.

reusable.what: Deterministically injects the grouped words + total duration + brand tokens into a 'caption skin' HTML template and self-lints it (brand-strict: no bare hex/rgb, canonical .caption-group/.caption-word hooks, window.__timelines key). This is the glue between grouping output and a renderable composition.
reusable.notes: PORT the buildPresetSkin() contract (lines 553-619): a skin HTML has 3 reserved holes — `var GROUPS = [];`, `var DURATION = 0;`, `<style data-brand-tokens></style>` and `data-duration="0"` — that you fill with JSON.stringify(groups), the real duration, and your brand CSS. In Python: read the skin .html, str.replace those 4 literals, write out. Skip the npm `hyperframes add` registry-install path. The self-lint regex set (lines 1095-1145) is a useful copy-paste QA gate.

reusable.what: A complete, self-contained, brand-token-strict kinetic/styled caption template (frosted-glass rounded pill, per-word karaoke highlight). Drop-in example of the 'richer styled captions (ASS/rounded-box/kinetic)' ClipPilot wants. ~50 sibling skins exist (block-frame, capsule, claude, neo-brutalism, retro-zine, 8-bit-orbit, etc.).
reusable.notes: The CSS IS the kinetic-caption recipe — reusable directly. Rounded box: `.caption-pill{padding:18px 40px 22px;border-radius:36px;backdrop-filter:blur(24px) saturate(1.3);border:1px solid var(--lg-rim-mid);box-shadow:inset 0 1px 0 var(--lg-rim-hi),0 18px 48px var(--lg-base-shadow-deep)}`. Per-word karaoke: `.caption-word.is-active{color:var(--ink-on-glass);background:linear-gradient(120deg,var(--brand-primary),var(--brand-secondary));border-radius:14px}`. Seek-safe animation uses gsap.set({className}) on a paused timeline (NOT .call callbacks). If ClipPilot stays ffmpeg/libass (no Chrome), translate these to ASS \an/\pos/\c/\bord/\shad + \t karaoke tags; if you adopt an HTML renderer, use as-is. Browse skins under skills\*\phases\design-system\style-presets\*\caption-skin.html and registry\components\caption-*\.

reusable.symbol: caption-kinetic-slam — per-word GSAP slam entrance (WORDS[], KEYWORDS set, fitFontSize())
reusable.what: Self-contained kinetic word-by-word caption block (Anton font, big uppercase, 4 rotating entrance modes: drop/slide-L/slide-R/scale-pop, gold keyword highlight). Pure CSS+GSAP, deterministic seek-safe timeline. The literal 'kinetic captions' asset named in the focus.
reusable.notes: Reusable recipe: 4 entrance variants chosen by `wi % 4` — drop `{y:-120,opacity:0}->{y:0,duration:0.22,ease:'back.out(1.7)'}`, slide `{x:±300}->{x:0,ease:'expo.out'}`, scale `{scale:0.4}->{scale:1,ease:'back.out(2.2)'}`. Includes a portable canvas-measureText `fitFontSize(text,base,weight,family,maxWidth)` shrink-to-fit helper (lines 99-110) and a deterministic mulberry32 PRNG (no Math.random — important: ClipPilot needs determinism too). 15 more siblings in registry\components\caption-* (glitch-rgb, neon-glow, pill-karaoke, gradient-fill, highlight, particle-burst, emoji-pop).

reusable.what: CPU-capable subject matting → RGBA video (WebM VP9-alpha / ProRes 4444), with an optional inverse-alpha background plate. Directly serves ClipPilot's NOT-YET-BUILT B-roll / subject-cutout stage.
reusable.notes: Model is u2net_human_seg (Apache-2.0, ~168MB auto-download, CPU EP — SKILL.md explicitly bans CoreML and runs CPU-only ~2fps@1080p). Key reusable ffmpeg ALPHA-ENCODE recipe (port to Python/onnxruntime + ffmpeg subprocess): decode frames → per-frame onnx alpha → encode with `-pix_fmt yuva420p -metadata:s:v:0 alpha_mode=1` (that metadata tag is what makes Chrome/browsers decode the alpha plane). QUALITY_CRF presets webm: fast=30/balanced=18/best=12. `backgroundOutputPath` yields a hole-cut plate (not inpainted) for compositing graphics behind the subject. Port the recipe; do not import the TS.

reusable.symbol: tts command — local Kokoro-82M ONNX TTS (DEFAULT_VOICE, 54 voices, --speed/--lang)
reusable.what: Local, offline, CPU TTS (Kokoro-82M via kokoro-onnx) producing narration .wav. Serves ClipPilot's Section B faceless-funnel GENERATION (script->TTS->...) without any paid API.
reusable.notes: kokoro-onnx is a separate MIT/Apache Python+ONNX package (github.com/thewh1teagle/kokoro-onnx, 54 voices) — ClipPilot can pip-install and call it DIRECTLY in Python (better than subprocessing the hyperframes CLI). HyperFrames TTS returns audio only (no word timings) for Kokoro/ElevenLabs; per skills\hyperframes-media\references\tts-to-captions.md you then run whisper to get word timings → feed into runGroup() above. (HeyGen provider returns words inline but is a paid API — reject that path.)

reusable.symbol: Claude-Code SKILL packages (embedded-captions, faceless-explainer, product-launch-video, pr-to-video)
reusable.what: Full agent SKILL.md playbooks for the exact pipelines ClipPilot is building: caption craft/placement rules, faceless explainer generation, and product-launch video. Reusable as design references and potentially as installed Claude-Code skills.
reusable.notes: embedded-captions\SKILL.md is the densest design reference: rail-vs-embed caption model, scarcity rules (<=1 hero per beat), occlusion/matte rules, the decision gate (refuse multi-speaker/no-face/burned-in-caption clips), WCAG contrast + determinism non-negotiables (no Math.random/Date.now/repeat:-1). Since ClipPilot already uses Claude-as-brain via MCP, these SKILL.md files can be adopted near-verbatim as system guidance. faceless-explainer\ is the structural blueprint for Section B.

best_for_clippilot: skills\faceless-explainer\scripts\captions.mjs — specifically the runGroup() function (lines 61-349). It is the deterministic whisper-words → styled-caption-groups engine ClipPilot is missing: cleaning, density-modulated grouping, silence/punctuation/scene splits, tail-pad clamping that guarantees no two captions overlap, and free all-caps/numeric emphasis tagging. It is pure list math (no Chrome, no ffmpeg, no GPU, no GPL), so it ports to Python almost line-for-line, and its sibling runHtml()/buildPresetSkin() plus the caption-skin.html templates give you the styled/kinetic render layer on top.
do_not_use: (1) Do NOT try to pip-import HyperFrames — it is Node.js/TypeScript, not a Python package; treat it as port/subprocess only. (2) Do NOT take the render ENGINE (packages/engine, producer) as code: it is headless-Chrome (Puppeteer) + multi-worker + Docker — heavy, Node-only, and ClipPilot already has its own real ffmpeg clip/caption/compose pipeline. Importing it duplicates what's built. (3) Do NOT use the HeyGen TTS provider path (tts-to-captions Path A) — it is a paid cloud API; use local Kokoro-onnx + whisper instead. (4) Do NOT use --gpu / --browser-gpu / WebGL shader-transitions paths — GPU-oriented and irrelevant to a CPU-only target. (5) Do NOT copy the npm-registry skin-install path in runHtml() (`npx hyperframes add caption-*`) — it needs network/registry; instead embed the caption-skin.html templates locally and fill the 3 reserved holes directly.

license: MIT License (Copyright (c) 2015 Zulko), confirmed in C:\Priyansh\Money making\moviepy-master\moviepy-master\LICENCE.txt. pyproject.toml restates "MIT License". Fully permissive for a closed-source, for-profit Windows app: import freely, keep the copyright notice in a NOTICES file.

reusable.what: Pure Pillow+numpy text-to-image renderer. Renders styled caption frames entirely with PIL ImageDraw.multiline_text (color, bg_color, stroke_color/stroke_width outline, per-line text_align, horizontal/vertical_align, interline spacing, margins, TTF font_size) and exposes the result as an RGBA numpy frame. method='caption' auto-wraps text to a fixed box and auto-fits font size via binary search (__find_optimum_font_size); method='label' auto-sizes the box to the text. NO ffmpeg and NO GPU at render time - the rendering is 100% CPU PIL. This is exactly the 'richer STYLED captions' primitive ClipPilot lacks: it produces transparent caption PNG frames you can either burn with your existing ffmpeg overlay path OR composite in-proc.
reusable.notes:
CPU-only, no cv2 (opencv is only imported by fx/Rotate,Resize,HeadBlur - not by TextClip). The core blit recipe to reuse even if you don't want the whole clip object: build the frame with PIL then take np.array(img). Excerpt of the load-bearing draw call:

img = Image.new('RGBA',(w,h),color=(0,0,0,0))
pil_font = ImageFont.truetype(font, font_size)
draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
draw.multiline_text(xy=(x,y), text=text, fill=color, font=pil_font, spacing=interline, align=text_align, stroke_width=stroke_width, stroke_fill=stroke_color, anchor='ls')
frame = np.array(img)  # RGBA, alpha is your caption mask

The 'ls' (left-baseline) anchor + y+=ascent trick (lines 1853-1865) is the key to predictable vertical placement with stroke - port that exactly. For a rounded-box karaoke look, draw.rounded_rectangle the bg before the text. To use as a clip: TextClip(font='C:/path/font.ttf', text=line, font_size=64, color='white', stroke_color='black', stroke_width=3, method='caption', size=(1080,None), text_align='center'). Gotcha: font is now a REQUIRED path to a .ttf/.otf (no fontconfig); ship a bundled font. Needs pillow>=9.2 (you likely already have it).

reusable.what: Lazy SRT-driven subtitle track: parses an .srt into [((start,end),text),...] and, via a user-supplied make_textclip(txt) factory, generates one styled TextClip per cue ONLY when that time is reached (memoized in self.textclips). Gives time-synced caption rendering for free if you feed it whisper output converted to SRT. file_to_subtitles is a standalone, dependency-light SRT parser you can lift verbatim.
reusable.notes: Port rather than import the whole class - it couples to moviepy's VideoClip frame model. The directly liftable part is the SRT parser (regex '([0-9]*:[0-9]*:[0-9]*,[0-9]*)' -> convert_to_seconds, blank-line delimits cues). The valuable design pattern: a make_textclip callback so ClipPilot picks the style per cue. If you adopt moviepy wholesale, use it as-is with your faster-whisper SRT: SubtitlesClip('cues.srt', make_textclip=lambda t: TextClip(font=..., text=t, method='caption', size=(1080,None), ...)). Only supports .srt (not .vtt).

reusable.what: In-proc layering (caption-over-video, logo/watermark overlays, lower-thirds) via pure-numpy alpha blit (frame_function lines 156-209, using clip.compose_on at VideoClip.py:720 which is pure numpy), and concatenate_videoclips for joining segments with method='chain' (fast, same-size) or method='compose' (auto-pad mismatched sizes) plus a transition clip and padding for crossfades. This is the moviepy equivalent of your ffmpeg overlay+concat.
reusable.notes: Reference/optional-import, do NOT replace your working ffmpeg compose path with this. moviepy compositing decodes/re-encodes through Python frame-by-frame, which on CPU is markedly slower than your existing ffmpeg-subprocess concat/overlay (filtergraph runs in native code). Keep ffmpeg for clip/compose. The one place CompositeVideoClip earns its keep: compositing a TextClip/overlay that has a true alpha mask onto frames where you'd otherwise fight ffmpeg overlay alpha. concatenate_videoclips(..., transition=xfade_clip, padding=-0.5) is a clean recipe to PORT into an ffmpeg xfade filtergraph rather than run in-proc. Note write step uses ffmpeg_write_video (imageio_ffmpeg bundled binary).

best_for_clippilot: moviepy/video/VideoClip.py :: TextClip class (C:\Priyansh\Money making\moviepy-master\moviepy-master\moviepy\video\VideoClip.py, lines 1559-2116). It is the single highest-value asset: a self-contained, CPU-only, ffmpeg-free Pillow+numpy styled-text renderer (stroke outline, bg box, alignment, auto-wrap, auto-fit font size) that outputs an RGBA frame whose alpha is a ready-made caption mask. Import moviepy and call TextClip directly, or port just the PIL multiline_text recipe (the 'ls' baseline anchor + ascent offset is the load-bearing detail) to produce transparent caption PNGs that ClipPilot burns via its existing ffmpeg overlay path. This delivers the missing 'richer styled captions (rounded-box/kinetic)' with no architectural change.
do_not_use: Do NOT route your video compose/concat/overlay through CompositeVideoClip / concatenate_videoclips for production output - moviepy composites by decoding+re-encoding frame-by-frame in Python, which is materially slower on CPU than your already-built ffmpeg-subprocess concat/overlay (native filtergraph). Use them only as design references or to generate alpha caption frames. Also avoid moviepy's fx that pull in opencv (video/fx/Rotate.py, Resize.py, HeadBlur.py import cv2) if you want to stay lean - they are not needed for the caption path. Nothing here is GPU-mandatory, paid, or GPL.

license: GNU GPL v3 (confirmed via C:\Priyansh\Money making\VideoCaptioner-master\VideoCaptioner-master\LICENSE, line 1-2: "GNU GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE Version 3, 29 June 2007"). Copyleft - we must NOT import/copy any .py source. Recipes only: ASS style strings, ffmpeg command/filtergraph shapes, and color-math formulas are facts/command strings, not copyrightable, so they may be re-authored (PORT).

reusable.what: Cleanest recipe in the repo for emitting a styled ASS [V4+ Styles] block from high-level params (font, primary/outline color, bold, spacing, outline width, margin). Two named styles Default+Secondary for bilingual. This is exactly the 'styled ASS subtitle' recipe ClipPilot needs for richer captions.
reusable.notes: GPL - re-author, do not import. Color hex->ASS &HAABBGGRR (note byte order is B,G,R not R,G,B): def hex_to_ass(h):h=h.lstrip('#'); if len(h)==8:a,r,g,b=h[0:2],h[2:4],h[4:6],h[6:8];return f'&H{a}{b}{g}{r}'; r,g,b=h[0:2],h[2:4],h[4:6];return f'&H00{b}{g}{r}'. Style line recipe (Format: Name,Fontname,Fontsize,PrimaryColour,SecondaryColour,OutlineColour,BackColour,Bold,Italic,Underline,StrikeOut,ScaleX,ScaleY,Spacing,Angle,BorderStyle,Outline,Shadow,Alignment,MarginL,MarginR,MarginV,Encoding): 'Style: Default,{font},{size},{primary},&H000000FF,{outline},&H00000000,{-1 if bold else 0},0,0,0,100,100,{spacing},0,1,{outline_width},0,2,10,10,{margin_bottom},1'. BorderStyle=1=outline; Alignment=2=bottom-center. Verified concrete preset values exist in resource\subtitle_style\ass-default.json (Noto Sans SC, size40, white #ffffff, black outline, width2.0, bold, spacing3.2, margin30) and ass-anime.json (orange outline #f58709, width2.6).

reusable.what: Recipe for assembling the full .ass file: [Script Info] with PlayResX/Y, the style block, [Events] Format header, and per-segment Dialogue lines. Also the bilingual layout trick (write Secondary line first so it renders below, Default after so it renders on top) and \n->\N newline conversion.

The ffmpeg burn-in filtergraph + the 720p-reference style auto-scaling recipe (_scale_ass_style scales Fontsize[2], Spacing[13], Outline[16], MarginV[21] by height/720). Shows the exact -vf ass=... invocation and Windows path escaping.
reusable.notes: GPL - re-author the command shape (filtergraph strings are not copyrightable). Burn filtergraph: -vf "ass='{ass_path}':fontsdir='{fonts_dir}'". Windows path escaping is load-bearing: ass_path = Path(p).as_posix().replace(':', '\\:') so 'C:/x.ass' -> 'C\\:/x.ass'. Full cmd: ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -acodec copy -vcodec libx264 -crf 23 -preset medium -vf ass='...':fontsdir='...' -y out.mp4. Scaling: parse Style: line, split(','), multiply parts[2],[13],[16],[21] by scale_factor. ClipPilot already has ffmpeg+libass burn per its built list, so this is mainly the fontsdir+escaping+scaling refinements.

reusable.what: Alternative TRUE rounded-box caption recipe that does NOT rely on libass: draws each subtitle as a transparent RGBA PNG with PIL draw.rounded_rectangle() background + centered text, then overlays PNGs onto video via ffmpeg overlay with enable='between(t,start,end)'. This is the rounded-box/box-highlight look that libass cannot do cleanly (BorderStyle=3 gives a rectangle, not rounded corners).
reusable.notes: GPL - re-author (it is pure PIL+ffmpeg, trivial to re-implement, CPU-only - perfect fit). Key calls: draw.rounded_rectangle([l,t,r,b], radius=corner_radius, fill=(r,g,b,a)); font.getbbox(text) for sizing; bg_width=max_line_width+padding_h*2, bg_height=total_text_height+padding_v*2. Overlay filtergraph per batch (BATCH_SIZE=50 to dodge ffmpeg input limits): chain '[0:v][1:v]overlay=0:0:enable=between(t,s,e)[v1];[v1][2:v]overlay=0:0:enable=...[v2]...' then -map [vN] -map 0:a? -t {duration}. Use -t {video_duration} to stop overlay from truncating. Concrete preset: rounded-default.json bg #0de3ffe5 (cyan, alpha e5), text #000000, radius14, padding_h18/v16. Style dataclass default RoundedBgStyle: bg #191919C8 (dark translucent), text #FFFFFF, radius12, padding 28x14, margin_bottom60. Needs PIL/Pillow (permissive).

reusable.what: Tiny helper supporting the rounded renderer: parses #RRGGBB and #RRGGBBAA into an RGBA tuple for PIL fills (so the translucent box alpha works).
reusable.notes: GPL but trivial fact: hex.lstrip('#'); 6 chars -> (r,g,b,255); 8 chars -> (r,g,b,a) via int(slice,16). Pair with the rounded_renderer port.

best_for_clippilot: The rounded-box caption recipe in C:\Priyansh\Money making\VideoCaptioner-master\VideoCaptioner-master\videocaptioner\core\subtitle\rounded_renderer.py (render_text_block + render_subtitle_image + render_rounded_video). PORT it. It is the one capability libass genuinely cannot produce - TRUE rounded-corner background boxes behind captions - and it is pure CPU PIL drawing (draw.rounded_rectangle) plus a simple ffmpeg overlay=enable='between(t,s,e)' filtergraph, which fits ClipPilot's CPU-only Windows constraint perfectly and complements the libass burn it already has. Second-best, take alongside it: style_manager.py to_ass_string()/_hex_to_ass() for the styled-ASS V4+ style-string recipe and the #RRGGBB->&HAABBGGRR color conversion (the byte-swap is the easy thing to get wrong).
do_not_use: Do NOT import or copy any .py files from this repo - it is GPL v3 and the app is closed-source for-profit; only re-author the recipes/command strings/ASS style lines (those are facts, not copyrightable). Do not pull in the PyQt5/qfluentwidgets UI layer (ui/view/subtitle_style_interface.py etc.) - it is desktop-GUI bloat irrelevant to a headless pipeline. Ignore the CUDA path in ass_renderer.render_ass_video (_check_cuda_available / -hwaccel cuda) - ClipPilot is CPU-only, just always use libx264 software encode. Skip the CJK-only auto-wrap machinery (ass_utils.auto_wrap_ass_file + font_utils get_ass_to_pil_ratio) unless you specifically need Chinese line-wrapping; for English, libass auto-wraps and the extra PIL font-ratio code is unnecessary complexity.

license: MIT (Copyright 2019 Stephen Macke; LICENSE file at repo root). The vendored Pipeline/TransformerMixin in ffsubsync/sklearn_shim.py are BSD-3-Clause (scikit-learn), attributed in-file. Both permissive and fine for a closed-source for-profit Windows app.

reusable.what: Language-agnostic FFT cross-correlation that finds the optimal integer-sample time offset between two binary speech-presence signals. This is the actual resync engine: given a reference signal and a subtitle-derived signal it returns (best_score, best_offset_samples). Pure numpy, CPU-only, no GPL deps. For ClipPilot this realigns an externally-edited/corrected caption SRT against the whisper-timed SRT (or against any reference signal) without re-running ASR.
reusable.notes: Core recipe (re-authorable but importable as MIT): map each signal to +/-1, zero-pad to next power of two, multiply FFTs, take argmax of real(ifft). Body: refstring,substring = map(lambda s: 2*np.array(s).astype(float)-1, [refstring,substring]); total_bits=math.log(len(substring)+len(refstring),2); total_length=int(2**math.ceil(total_bits)); extra_zeros=total_length-len(substring)-len(refstring); subft=np.fft.fft(np.append(np.zeros(extra_zeros+len(refstring)),substring)); refft=np.fft.fft(np.flip(np.append(refstring,np.zeros(len(substring)+extra_zeros)),0)); convolve=np.real(np.fft.ifft(subft*refft)); best_idx=int(np.argmax(convolve)); best_offset=len(convolve)-1-best_idx-len(substring); best_score=convolve[best_idx]. max_offset_samples clamps implausible offsets (set = max_offset_seconds*SAMPLE_RATE). Deps: numpy only. Raises FailedToFindAlignmentException on empty input.

reusable.what: Converts a parsed SRT into the 100 Hz binary speech-presence numpy vector that FFTAligner consumes. Rasterizes each cue: samples[start:end]=1.0 over [sub.start, sub.end] at SAMPLE_RATE=100. _is_metadata drops music-note/bracketed/non-dialogue cues so they do not pollute the signal. This is how you turn ANY .srt (reference or input) into an alignable signal with zero ffmpeg/audio.
reusable.notes: Rasterization core: samples=np.zeros(int(max_time*sample_rate)+2,dtype=float); for sub in subs: if _is_metadata(...): continue; start=int(round((sub.start.total_seconds()-start_seconds)*sample_rate)); end=start+int(round((sub.end.total_seconds()-sub.start.total_seconds())*sample_rate)); samples[start:end]=min(1.0/framerate_ratio,1.0). SAMPLE_RATE=100 (constants.py) => 10ms resolution; offset_seconds = offset_samples/100. Deps: numpy, srt, pysubs2. NOTE: importing speech_transformers.py top-level also defines audio/VAD transformers, but auditok(GPL)/webrtcvad are imported lazily INSIDE their functions, so importing this module for the SRT path pulls in NO GPL code.

reusable.what: Top-level orchestration of an SRT-vs-SRT resync. run() takes an argparse.Namespace, validates, and returns {'sync_was_successful','offset_seconds','framerate_scale_factor','retval'}. try_sync builds srt_pipes, calls MaxScoreAligner(FFTAligner,...).fit_transform(reference_signal, srt_pipes), applies SubtitleShifter(offset_seconds), and writes output. The SRT-only branch is reached when reference is itself a subtitle file (see make_reference_pipe -> make_subtitle_speech_pipeline when _ref_format in SUBTITLE_EXTENSIONS).
reusable.notes: Cleanest programmatic entry: build a Namespace (or call ffsubsync.make_parser()), set reference=<reference.srt>, srtin=[<input.srt>], srtout=<out.srt>, no_fix_framerate=True (skip framerate search for same-source captions), vad=None; then result=ffsubsync.run(args). offset_seconds=offset_samples/float(SAMPLE_RATE)+args.apply_offset_seconds (ffsubsync.py:239). assess_alignment_quality()+--skip-sync-on-low-quality (min_score/quality_max_offset/max_framerate_deviation) lets you reject a bad alignment and keep the original SRT - useful for batch ClipPilot jobs. Recommend referencing this flow but calling FFTAligner + SubtitleSpeechTransformer directly to avoid the argparse surface.

reusable.what: Applies the discovered offset to every cue (timedelta add) producing the resynced subtitle set; GenericSubtitlesFile.write_file then emits srt/ass/vtt. The actual shift step after alignment.
reusable.notes: SubtitleShifter.__init__ wraps seconds in timedelta; fit: self.subs_=subs.offset(self.td_seconds). offset() in generic_subtitles.py: [GenericSubtitle(sub.start+td, sub.end+td, sub.inner) for sub in subs]. write_file supports srt and (via pysubs2) ass/ssa/vtt, so ClipPilot can resync then re-emit styled ASS. Deps: srt, pysubs2.

reusable.what: Robust encoding-detecting SRT/ASS/SSA/VTT parser (tries cchardet/charset_normalizer/chardet) with max-subtitle-duration clamping and tolerant error handling. Useful standalone for ingesting arbitrary user-supplied .srt before alignment.
reusable.notes: fmt='srt' -> srt.parse(decoded, ignore_errors=not strict); fmt in ('ass','ssa','sub','vtt') -> pysubs2.SSAFile.from_string. Encoding 'infer' loops cchardet/charset_normalizer/chardet. Deps: srt>=3.0.0, pysubs2>=1.2.0, charset_normalizer (chardet optional, faust-cchardet only on py<3.13 so on Python 3.14 it falls back to charset_normalizer - fine).

best_for_clippilot: ffsubsync/aligners.py -> FFTAligner (with ffsubsync/speech_transformers.py:946 SubtitleSpeechTransformer to rasterize the SRT). Together they are a self-contained, MIT, CPU-only, pure-numpy SRT-resync core: rasterize two SRTs to 100 Hz binary signals, FFT-cross-correlate, get offset_seconds = best_offset/100. Import these two (plus subtitle_parser + SubtitleShifter) to realign an edited/corrected caption SRT to ClipPilot's whisper timing without re-running ASR, and to snap externally-supplied .srt onto a clip.
do_not_use: Do NOT touch the audio/video reference paths: VideoSpeechTransformer / MultiSegmentVideoSpeechTransformer / _make_auditok_detector / _make_webrtcvad_detector in speech_transformers.py. auditok==0.1.5 is GPLv3 (the repo itself warns importing it creates a GPLv3 derivative work - poison for a closed-source for-profit app) and webrtcvad pulls native VAD; both are unnecessary for the SRT-to-SRT focus. Stick to subtitle-only inputs (reference must be a .srt/.ass), pass vad=None and avoid --vad. Also skip the GUI (ffsubsync_gui.py), PGS/ffmpeg subtitle-extraction (extract_subtitles_from_reference), and the framerate-ratio search for same-source captions (set no_fix_framerate=True) since both sides share whisper timing.

license: MIT License (LICENSE.txt: Copyright (c) 2021 Johnathan Nader et al.; setup.py license="MIT"). Fully permissive — import, port, subprocess, or sell all allowed with attribution.

reusable.what: Mattes the video subject (U2-Net alpha) and composites it, centered, over a still image background. This is exactly ClipPilot's B-roll/subject-cutout-over-background need: speaker cut out, dropped onto a generated backdrop.
reusable.notes: MIT. import-time line 15 `multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn', force=True)` clashes with PySide6/Qt, so DO NOT import — run via CLI: `backgroundremover -i clip.mp4 -toi -bi bg.png -o out.mov --alpha-codec prores_ks`. The load-bearing part is the final ffmpeg filtergraph (filtergraphs are not copyrightable, so you may also PORT this string directly onto ClipPilot's own matte without the package): matte_key produces a grayscale mask mp4, then: ffmpeg -i bg.jpg -i clip.mp4 -i matte.mp4 -filter_complex "[2][1]scale2ref[mask][main];[main][mask]alphamerge[fg];[0:v]scale2ref[bg][fg];[bg][fg]overlay=(W-w)/2:(H-h)/2:shortest=1[out]" -map [out] -map 1:a? -shortest out.mov . Note it first normalizes the bg image with `scale2ref[img][vid];[img]setsar=1;[vid]nullsink`.

reusable.what: Produces a true alpha-channel video (transparent background) of the subject — a reusable overlay asset ClipPilot can composite over ANY generated B-roll/backdrop later, not just a fixed image.
reusable.notes: MIT. The reusable recipe is the alpha-merge + codec selection. Core filtergraph (port directly): `[1][0]scale2ref[mask][main];[main][mask]alphamerge[v]` -map [v] -map 0:a? -shortest. The codec logic in _alpha_encoding_args is the genuinely useful, easy-to-miss part for Windows CPU output: .webm => libvpx-vp9 args `['-c:v','libvpx-vp9','-crf','30','-b:v','0','-pix_fmt','yuva420p']`; otherwise ProRes 4444 `['-c:v','prores_ks','-profile:v','4','-pix_fmt','yuva444p10le']`; qtrle option => `['-c:v','qtrle','-pix_fmt','argb']`. Use libvpx-vp9/yuva420p for web-friendly transparent overlays; prores_ks is large but CPU-fast and lossless-ish.

reusable.what: Single-image subject cutout via U2-Net producing RGBA PNG; supports background_color, background_image compositing, only_mask (returns the matte), and mask_threshold for hard edges. Useful for cutout thumbnails / static B-roll subject stills.
reusable.notes: MIT but pulls heavy torch+pymatting+u2net deps and downloads a ~175MB .pth on first run; runs CPU-only fine (bg.py lines 42-44 fall back to torch.device('cpu')). Because of the same module-level spawn() in utilities (imported transitively) and Qt clash, prefer subprocess: `backgroundremover -i in.png -o out.png` (add `-om` for mask-only, `-mt 128` hard edges, `-bc '0,255,0'` for greenscreen fill, `-bi bg.png` to composite). Per-frame mask is only_mask=True returning PNG buffer. Model auto-downloads to ~/.u2net/.

reusable.what: Composites the matted subject over a background VIDEO (e.g. a generated motion B-roll loop behind the speaker).
reusable.notes: MIT. Filtergraph to port: `[1][0]scale2ref[mask][main];[main][mask]alphamerge[vid];[vid][2:v]scale2ref[fg][bg];[bg][fg]overlay[out]` -map [out] -map 2:a? -shortest. Same spawn/Qt caveat — port the recipe or subprocess the CLI (`-tov -bv background.mp4`).

best_for_clippilot: transparentvideooverimage() in C:\Priyansh\Money making\backgroundremover-main\backgroundremover-main\backgroundremover\utilities.py — it is the end-to-end subject-cutout-over-background-B-roll capability ClipPilot lacks. Take it as a subprocess CLI call (`backgroundremover -i clip.mp4 -toi -bi bg.png -o out.mov`), and port the bare alphamerge+overlay filtergraph onto ClipPilot's own ffmpeg pipeline so you skip the heavy import entirely.
do_not_use: Do NOT import the package in-process: utilities.py line 15 `multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn', force=True)` runs at import time and clashes with PySide6/Qt's event loop (the documented spawn clash) — always subprocess it. Do NOT depend on the GPU/CUDA paths (bg.py max_workers VRAM math, DEVICE=cuda, torch.jit.trace on GPU) — ClipPilot is CPU-only; the CPU fallback works but multi-worker frame ripping is slow and memory-heavy. Avoid re-harvesting bg.py's U2-Net inference itself if ClipPilot already has a matting/vision path; the value here is the ffmpeg alpha compositing recipes, not the model. Note the ~175MB model auto-download and torch/pymatting dependency weight before bundling.

reusable.symbol: buildNanoBananaPrompt() + CAMERA_MAP / LENS_MAP / FOCAL_PERSPECTIVE / APERTURE_EFFECT dicts (lines 8-105)
reusable.what: Pure, dependency-free prompt-templating function: takes a base scene prompt + cinematography selections (camera, lens, focal length, aperture) and concatenates them with fixed quality tags into a rich text-to-image prompt. No MuAPI/network/React inside this function - plain data + string-join. Reusable for ClipPilot Section B (faceless funnel) and Section C (ad-share) to turn a scene beat into a high-quality image/video prompt for the vision brain or any T2I/T2V backend.
reusable.notes: Signature: buildNanoBananaPrompt(basePrompt, camera, lens, focalLength, aperture). Re-author in Python (~25 lines). The 4 maps are the value (real cinematography vocabulary). Recipe: parts=[basePrompt, f'shot on a {CAMERA_MAP[camera]}', f'using a {LENS_MAP[lens]} at {focal}mm ({FOCAL_PERSPECTIVE[focal]})', f'aperture {aperture}', APERTURE_EFFECT[aperture], 'cinematic lighting', 'natural color science', 'high dynamic range', 'professional photography, ultra-detailed, 8K resolution']; return ', '.join(p for p in parts if p and p.strip()). Sample data: APERTURE_EFFECT={'f/1.4':'shallow depth of field, creamy bokeh','f/4':'balanced depth of field','f/11':'deep focus clarity, sharp foreground to background'}; FOCAL_PERSPECTIVE={35:'natural cinematic perspective',85:'classic portrait perspective'}. Pure data/string = copyright-safe to port.

reusable.what: Generic async 'submit job -> poll predictions endpoint until terminal status' client recipe used by every studio. POST returns request_id; loop GET /predictions/{id}/result every 2s up to maxAttempts; status in {completed,succeeded,success}=done, {failed,error}=fail, HTTP 5xx=retryable; normalize output via result.outputs[0]||result.url||result.output?.url. A reference shape if ClipPilot ever wraps a hosted (non-CPU) generation backend behind its existing SQLite job engine.
reusable.notes: ClipPilot already owns a SQLite job engine + real ffmpeg/faster-whisper, so do NOT adopt this as code - only mirror the terminal-status set and the outputs[0]||url||output.url normalization when wrapping a future polled provider. Video/audio use maxAttempts=900, interval=2000 (30 min ceiling). All calls hit api.muapi.ai with header x-api-key (PAID) - port the control-flow idea only, never the endpoint.

best_for_clippilot: packages/studio/src/components/CinemaStudio.jsx - the buildNanoBananaPrompt() function plus its CAMERA_MAP/LENS_MAP/FOCAL_PERSPECTIVE/APERTURE_EFFECT cinematography dictionaries (lines 8-105). It is the only self-contained, network-free, copyright-safe piece of real IP in the repo: a ready-made recipe for converting a scene description into a rich cinematic image/video prompt. Port it to Python (~25 lines) to power visual-prompt generation in ClipPilot Section B and C.
do_not_use: Do NOT take any *Studio.jsx generation path or src/lib/muapi.js / packages/studio/src/muapi.js network code (generateMarketingStudioAd, generateAudio, generateImage/Video/I2V, runClipping, WorkflowStudio engine): every one is a thin wrapper over the PAID MuAPI aggregator (https://api.muapi.ai, x-api-key) - flag MuAPI as a paid dependency to avoid. There is NO real social publishing here: 'publish'/getPublishedWorkflows is just a community-templates tab, and the repo has zero YouTube/Instagram/Facebook/TikTok or OAuth uploader code (grep confirmed). 'Generative-Media-Skills' is an EXTERNAL GitHub repo only linked from README/McpCliStudio.jsx, not bundled - nothing local to harvest for it. UploadPicker.js / uploadHistory.js are browser-only DOM/localStorage widgets (not portable to PySide6) and their 'upload' is MuAPI asset hosting, not cross-posting. The whole repo is JS/React/Electron - no Python, no CPU-only model code.

license: DUAL / SOLO-LICENSED. Repo-root C:\Priyansh\Money making\remotion-main\remotion-main\LICENSE.md is the custom "Remotion License" (NOT OSI): free for individuals, non-profits, and for-profit orgs with up to 3 employees; a paid Company License from remotion.pro is REQUIRED for larger for-profit entities. Explicitly disallows copying/modifying Remotion to sell a derivative. SEPARATELY, the @remotion/captions sub-package (packages/captions/package.json) declares "license": "MIT" with zero dependencies — its pure-TS algorithms are MIT and safe to port. The render RUNTIME (remotion, @remotion/cli, studio) is solo-licensed, not MIT.

reusable.what: Pure function that groups word/token-level caption entries into karaoke 'pages' with per-token from/to timings, the core algorithm for word-by-word highlighted (kinetic) captions. Input: Caption[] {text,startMs,endMs,timestampMs,confidence} + combineTokensWithinMilliseconds. Output: {pages:[{text,startMs,durationMs,tokens:[{text,fromMs,toMs}]}]}. This is exactly the data structure ClipPilot needs to drive an ASS karaoke (\k) burn or a per-word highlight overlay.
reusable.notes: MIT, no deps, ~70 lines. Port to Python. Core rule: a new page starts when text.startsWith(' ') AND (currentTo-currentFrom) > combineTokensWithinMilliseconds; otherwise append token. First token of a page is trimStart()'d; durationMs of page N is set to pages[N+1].startMs - pages[N].startMs (last page = currentTo-startMs). Typical combineTokensWithinMilliseconds=1200 (one short phrase per page) per template-tiktok index.tsx SWITCH_CAPTIONS_EVERY_MS. The {fromMs,toMs} per token map directly to ASS \kf karaoke or to a frame range where you swap a word's color to the highlight (template uses HIGHLIGHT_COLOR=#39E508).

reusable.what: Pure line-wrapping for captions: splits captions into individual words then packs them into segments under maxCharsPerLine, with orphan-word prevention so a line doesn't end with 1 dangling word. Needed so burned captions never overflow the 1080-wide 9:16 frame.
reusable.notes: MIT, no deps. Algorithm: splitWords prefixes a leading space on the first word of each source caption. Then accumulate words while sum(len) stays <= maxCharsPerLine; break when adding next word exceeds maxCharsPerLine OR preventOrphanWord = (remainingWords.length<4 && >1 && filledCharacters>maxCharsPerLine/2). Returns {segments: Caption[][]}, each inner array = one line. Pair with serializeSrt to emit a wrapped .srt for ffmpeg subtitles= burn-in.

reusable.what: Serializes Caption[][] (lines) into a valid .srt string with HH:MM:SS,mmm timestamps. Directly produces the subtitle file ClipPilot can hand to ffmpeg's subtitles/ass filter for burn-in.
reusable.notes: MIT, no deps, ~40 lines. Timestamp formatter: hours=floor(ms/3600000); minutes=floor((ms%3600000)/60000); seconds=floor((ms%60000)/1000); millis=floor(ms%1000); format `${HH}:${MM}:${SS},${mmm}` zero-padded (millis to 3). Each block = index\nSTART --> END\ntext, joined by blank lines. Trivial Python rewrite.

reusable.what: Parses an .srt string back into Caption[] (startMs/endMs/timestampMs). Useful if ClipPilot ingests externally-supplied SRTs or round-trips after manual edits.
reusable.notes: MIT, no deps. toSeconds splits 'HH:MM:SS,mmm' and computes h*3600+m*60+s+mmm/1000. Sets confidence=1, timestampMs=midpoint. Minor: assumes \n line endings, so normalize \r\n first in the Python port.

reusable.what: Visual spec for the TikTok/Reels kinetic caption look: bold uppercase, white fill with thick black stroke (paintOrder stroke), spring scale+translateY entrance, active word swapped to green highlight. Defines the exact styling ClipPilot should replicate when authoring an ASS style.
reusable.notes: Do NOT import — needs the solo-licensed Remotion runtime + headless Chrome (heavy, GPU-leaning, license trap). Use as a DESIGN spec to author an equivalent ASS style for ffmpeg libass burn (ClipPilot already does libass). Recipe to replicate: font 'TheBoldFont' bold, DESIRED_FONT_SIZE=120 px clamped via fit-to-width(0.9*W), color white, WebkitTextStroke 20px black -> ASS Outline≈8-10 with OutlineColour &H000000 and BorderStyle=1, textTransform uppercase, HIGHLIGHT active token #39E508 -> ASS \1c&H08E539& (BGR) via \kf karaoke. ASS style line e.g.: 'Style: Pop,TheBoldFont,120,&H00FFFFFF,&H0008E539,&H00000000,&H00000000,-1,0,0,0,100,100,0,0,1,9,0,2,40,40,120,1'. Active-word timing comes straight from the token fromMs/toMs of createTikTokStyleCaptions.

reusable.what: Reference wiring showing how subtitles.json (Caption[]) -> createTikTokStyleCaptions(combineTokensWithinMilliseconds=1200) -> per-page Sequences. Confirms recommended grouping window and the .mp4->.json sidecar convention.
reusable.notes: Runtime-bound (Remotion), reference only. Key constants to carry over: SWITCH_CAPTIONS_EVERY_MS=1200; comment notes 1500=many words/page, 200=one word/page — gives ClipPilot a tunable knob for caption density.

reusable.what: End-to-end faceless-caption pipeline reference: extract 16kHz mono wav via ffmpeg, whisper.cpp transcribe with tokenLevelTimestamps+splitOnWord, write Caption[] JSON sidecar next to the video. Mirrors what ClipPilot already does but confirms the exact whisper flags for word-level karaoke timing.
reusable.notes: sub.mjs file is UNLICENSED template code — use as recipe only, do not copy file. ffmpeg audio-prep string is not copyrightable: `ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -ar 16000 out.wav -y` (16kHz wav for whisper). Whisper flags that matter for kinetic captions: tokenLevelTimestamps:true + splitOnWord:true -> per-word from/to offsets that feed createTikTokStyleCaptions. ClipPilot already has faster-whisper; just ensure word_timestamps=True to get the same token granularity.

best_for_clippilot: packages/captions/src/create-tiktok-style-captions.ts — the createTikTokStyleCaptions algorithm. It is MIT, dependency-free, ~70 lines, and produces exactly the page+per-token {fromMs,toMs} timing structure needed to drive karaoke/kinetic styled captions (ASS \kf or per-word color swap) in ClipPilot's existing ffmpeg libass burn. Port it to Python and feed it faster-whisper word timestamps.
do_not_use: Do NOT import or ship the Remotion render RUNTIME (the `remotion`, `@remotion/cli`, `@remotion/studio` packages or `npx remotion render`/`remotion bundle`): the repo-root LICENSE.md is solo/company-licensed (paid Company License required for non-tiny for-profits and selling a derivative is disallowed) — a legal + cost trap for a closed-source for-profit app, and it renders via headless Chrome which is heavy and GPU-leaning. Do NOT import the JSX caption components (Page.tsx, SubtitlePage.tsx, BoxedSingleCaption SingleCaption.tsx) — they require that runtime; treat them as visual/design reference only. The MIT exception applies ONLY to the @remotion/captions sub-package's pure-TS algorithms, which you port, not import.
